chapters.title: The serious business of game making
chapters.text:
Video games are built to bring joy. Their whole point is to be a pastime that teaches a little and amuses a lot. The common view is that games are always a great time, so we have come to believe that players and makers share in the fun.

The play side is fun, but the making side is not the same.

The idea that games are all fun holds for players, even the ones stuck on a level for days or weeks. For the makers, it is not so fun, and it is hard work. They pour in endless hours of coding and debugging so you and I can sit back and enjoy a sharp piece of work.

Beyond the code side, there is a wider and richer business side. Games have cultural reach and pop culture weight. A game, like any piece of art, pays tribute to its setting, the maker's voice, or the feel of the era it was made in.

The trouble of making a game is not only in the code. Real world problems run deeper.

The game has to make money. The hours put into any title need staff and tools, which cost a lot. So, those costs have to be paid back by sales, which sends makers into overdrive, pushing the game to do well in the market.

A game is no good if it only brings joy and leaves the makers broke and in debt.

We will not get ahead of ourselves. You will see more as the summary unfolds. This summary is for players who want to know what goes on behind the curtain, and for business minded people who want to make their mark in gaming.

In the next chapters, you will see the huge amount of work that goes into building games people enjoy. Along the way, you will also pick up ideas for building your own gaming business if that is your path.

A short note, one sure way to bother a game developer is to ask, after you learn what they do for a living, what it is like to spend all day playing games.

chapters.title: How expectation can hold a team back
chapters.text:
At the start of every project, a wave of expectations shows up. Everyone hopes it does well with players and in the market. Goals and targets are useful tools if you want your project to thrive for a long time. But they can also hurt.

Sometimes tight goals are the trap of a strong project.

When a game has a hit prequel or a legacy it has to live up to, the team goes into crisis mode, dotting every i and crossing every t, but at times mixing them up. That leads to bugs or weak play, like broken scenes or muddled plots. The pressure can force the team into slips.

It is key for makers to work in a calm space to avoid errors.

Goals and expectations matter, but they should be real and healthy. The weight of a past win can break future projects. Oddly, a past win should fuel the next one, so we have to be careful not to burn out trying to match it. The buzz around a hit can push a sequel out the door too fast, which leads to a cold reception. That move is usually meant to cash in on the hit, but it can wreck the whole series.

Games are a tricky business, and they may not suit everyone. The key is to note that you can always redesign to meet public demand.

Makers have to study the market carefully before adding sequels to hit games. A market analyst can help here.

This is not just a game lesson. It is a business lesson that fits any field.

First, do not let a past win get in your head and stop you from aiming higher. As we have just seen, nothing kills future work like sitting on past wins.

Second, the fact that the market cheers you on does not mean they are ready for a sequel, or that the sequel you make will be a hit. Do your homework.

chapters.title: The power of working as a team
chapters.text:
A project carries a lot of weight, and making a game is no different. Many parts go into a big game team, from coders to animators to debuggers and so on. Each part matters. Skipping one is to doom the whole project before it even starts.

A hit game stands on the strength of the team behind it.

So, teamwork is a key and irreplaceable factor in game design and execution. Still, collaboration alone cannot finish the job without the right money. Without funds to kick off the project, the whole thing never gets off the ground. The money side is often ignored or downplayed because it is not part of the cool software side. But make no mistake, it is the lifeblood of the work.

A team stays happy and focused when there is a steady flow of money to make the project real.

The lead has to find a way to give everyone a sense of ownership and equal weight in the group and the project. Once your team is working as one, you can take on any challenge with the assurance that you will pull through.

When every member of the team buys into the game's success, the odds of a win go up.

Projects can also be done solo. Eric Barone, for example, spent six years of twelve hour days alone, with the support of his girlfriend, to build the quiet farming game Stardew Valley. He took on a task that would have taken a whole team and came out the other side. Still, this does not take away from the strength of teamwork, since a team would not need six years to finish a single game that simple.

A short note, Master Chief in Halo is voiced by radio DJ Steve Downes.

chapters.title: A bug is not the end of the road
chapters.text:
Game making brings the risk of errors and glitches that can undo hours of work. A bug is an error, flaw, or fault in a program or system that makes it produce a wrong or strange result, or behave in ways you did not plan. A bug can come from a small slip in one or more lines of code.

It is key to note that there is always a fix, no matter the hitch in the code.

A bug can end a rising game design or push the finish line far out. The team will feel a real dip in mood and energy when they hit a bug. Picture this, after weeks of coding, you are getting ready for the test phase. Then, a blinking error message hits you. It is frustrating.

One small bug can ruin a whole project if it is left alone, so it is key to debug as soon as a bug shows up.

Coding is a key part of any software, and it is wise to keep a tight focus on detail when writing code, to cut down on errors. Doing so keeps you from hitting these walls after weeks of work.

A bug should not stop you. You can always debug and finish.

A bug can ruin a well made game and make all your work useless. The good news is a bug is just a line of code, and it can be fixed with time and effort in a process called debugging. Debugging is the method of finding and fixing bugs in software. Since debugging makes the project run longer than planned, the release date has to slide, but it can be done.

chapters.title: The small team that beat the giants
chapters.text:
Hot off the success of their last releases, a small Polish studio was getting ready to drop the third part of their hit series, The Witcher III. CD Projekt Red is a small studio out of Poland with a modest name in the game world. With the release of Witcher III, they locked in their place as a giant.

On average, a regular role playing game takes about twenty hours to finish. Projekt Red did the unthinkable. They built a map so rich and so wide that a fast player needed two hundred hours to get through, and a slow player even longer. It is stunning that they made a real world with a huge map that did not fall short of what players wanted.

Some games sell on the strength of the studio's name, and that is why bigger brands move more units.

You would expect this kind of feat from the big names in the field like Electronic Arts, but this small studio did what the giants had not even dreamed of. Their small size and quiet name made the win all the more striking, and the game spoke for them, loud and clear.

It pays to keep an eye on the dark horse and put your money on it. It might outrun the safe bets.

Investors wish they had spotted the spark in such a small player, but it pays to keep an eye on these teams.

chapters.title: Not every story ends with a win
chapters.text:
For all the stories around making and shipping great games across the many decades since the first one, there are always tales of struggle and hardship that end in a win. Super Mario, The Witcher III, and even the solo project Stardew Valley were all marked by hurdles, bugs, debugging, and long hours of hard work, but in the end, a great win.

Many of the games we play today came from real struggle and sometimes at a high personal cost.

We would not have the games we play if the makers had failed or quit when the bugs piled up. We expect the games to end well, and of course, they do, often with you reclaiming your throne or taking down your lifelong foe.

Life does not always play out like our favorite games, and it can end unhappily.

But that is only part of the story. For every hit, a hundred others never made it past testing. Many game ideas were trashed in a board meeting, called unplayable, predictable, or too dull by testers, or just died as ideas. That is the fate of many game projects.

We should value the work that went into the games that never saw the light of day.

As we praise the makers of the games we sit and enjoy for hours, we should not forget the hard work that went into the ones that never made it. The pitches that fell flat, the bugs that would not quit, and the files that got deleted by mistake and ruined a great dream.

A short note, one of the biggest issues with long game development is that, when you playtest for too long, you start inventing problems and adding layers that the game does not need.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Blood, Sweat, and Pixels peels back the curtain on what it really takes to ship a game, the forces that decide whether a project sinks or swims, and the moves to make when the wall shows up. The gaming world has handed humanity a long stretch of joy. Some titles have been reborn as films, and some films have been rebuilt as titles.

You cannot overstate the cultural footprint of games. They have nudged the norm with daring designs and plots. These games often borrow from a deep well of history to retell tales that land with many people. Some are a lens on the present, lifting a line from today's headlines and turning it into a scene, while others sketch how we imagine the future, borrowing a line from pop culture and putting it on screen.

Tales of games and how they were built give readers a sense of walking the road with the makers. With that sense, readers can also honor the hours people pour into the games they love, in one way or another. Without Jason Schreier's sharp nose for story, readers may never have stepped past the screens and pixels to glimpse the bare truth of game making. The sleepless nights, the long shifts, and the bugs that grind the gears and force a redo all stay hidden in the final product.

A short note, sorting the strands of a good game story is like fitting a well built puzzle. After a long, sometimes hard road, the pieces snap into place, and you get the quiet reward of work done right.

A small step to try.

If you want to step into the game world as a business person, or simply build games for the love of it, start with the starter languages below.

Pick up C plus plus, then Java, then C sharp.

If you have read this far, you have a real sense of what it takes.
